summary:
The attempted coup against Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev on August 19–22, 1991 paradoxically accelerated the collapse of the USSR it was intended to prevent. A self-styled State Committee for the State of Emergency, composed of hardline Communist Party officials, KGB chief Vladimir Kryuchkov, and Defense Minister Dmitry Yazov, announced that Gorbachev was ill and that they were assuming power. Tanks appeared in Moscow, and Gorbachev was placed under house arrest at his dacha in Crimea.

The coup collapsed within three days. Russian President Boris Yeltsin climbed atop a tank outside the Russian parliament building, rallied popular resistance, and became the symbol of democratic defiance. Crucial military units refused to fire on civilians, KGB Alpha Group declined to storm the parliament, and the coup plotters lost their nerve. Gorbachev returned to Moscow, but his authority was mortally damaged. The coup demonstrated the impossibility of holding the USSR together by force, accelerated the independence declarations of Soviet republics, and directly led to the formal [[event:2682|dissolution of the Soviet Union]] in December 1991.
